problem_summary: Servo emits continuous buzzing noise near specific joint angles, indicating PWM range mismatch, mechanical binding, or power supply instability.
visible_observations: Continuous buzzing sound near certain position command values
Noise correlates with specific joint angles, not continuous motion
Servo arm appears to oscillate slightly rather than hold position

likely_causes.description: Mechanical binding at specific joint angles causing servo to hunt
likely_causes.evidence: Buzzing occurs only near certain angles — servo is fighting physical resistance

likely_causes.description: PWM pulse width outside manufacturer-specified 1000–2000µs range
likely_causes.evidence: Noise correlates with position command values near software min/max limits

likely_causes.description: Power supply voltage drop under load causing servo to lose position
likely_causes.evidence: Buzzing intensifies under mechanical load, quieter when unloaded

likely_causes.description: Internal gear damage causing rough movement and acoustic noise
likely_causes.evidence: Grinding texture mixed with buzz, felt when manually rotating horn with power off

clarifying_questions: Does the buzzing occur only at specific joint angles, or is it continuous throughout the full range?
What PWM library and pulse range are you using — e.g., 500–2500µs or 1000–2000µs?
Does the noise change or stop when you physically remove the mechanical load from the servo horn?
tools_needed: Caliper (to check for binding in mechanical linkage)
next_best_test: Remove all mechanical load from the servo horn and command the servo to the angle where buzzing occurs. If buzzing stops, the cause is mechanical binding; if it continues, the cause is electrical or software.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Disconnect the servo from all mechanical linkage. Command it to the buzzing angle using a servo tester.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Buzzing stops — confirms mechanical binding as the cause. Buzzing continues — confirms electrical or software issue.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Measure supply voltage at the servo power leads under load using a multimeter.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Do not short the probes against the signal wire — only measure V+ and GND.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Voltage stays above 4.8V for a 5V servo. Drop below 4.5V indicates PSU fault.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Check your software PWM pulse range against the servo datasheet. Adjust min/max to the rated range (typically 1000–2000µs).
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Servo moves smoothly to all commanded positions without buzzing at limits.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: With power off, manually rotate the servo horn through its full range. Feel for any grinding, stiffness, or rough spots.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Smooth rotation with no rough spots — gears are intact. Grinding indicates internal gear damage.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: If mechanical binding was found, adjust linkage geometry or add lubrication. Re-test under load.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Use only servo-safe lubricant. Do not use WD-40 on plastic gears.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Servo holds position without buzzing at all commanded angles.

stop_conditions: Stop immediately if the servo case is hot to the touch — indicates internal short or stall overcurrent.
Stop if supply voltage drops below 4.5V under test — PSU fault, risk of brownout cascade.
Stop if grinding sound is accompanied by visible plastic debris — gear strip, servo must be replaced.
prevention: Always define software PWM min/max limits to the servo's rated pulse range before full-range operation.
Add current monitoring to the servo power rail to detect stall conditions before physical damage occurs.
Verify mechanical linkage alignment and range of motion before first power-on after assembly.

retrieved_sources.title: Servo Motor Calibration and PWM Range Guide
